NKJV.1: These also are proverbs of Solomon which the men of Hezekiah king of Judah copied:
NKJV.2: It is the glory of God to conceal a matter, But the glory of kings is to search out a matter.
NKJV.3: As the heavens for height and the earth for depth, So the heart of kings is unsearchable.
NKJV.4: Take away the dross from silver, And it will go to the silversmith for jewelry.
NKJV.5: Take away the wicked from before the king, And his throne will be established in righteousness.
NKJV.6: Do not exalt yourself in the presence of the king, And do not stand in the place of the great;
NKJV.7: For it is better that he say to you, “Come up here,” Than that you should be put lower in the presence of the prince, Whom your eyes have seen.
NKJV.8: Do not go hastily to court; For what will you do in the end, When your neighbor has put you to shame?
NKJV.9: Debate your case with your neighbor, And do not disclose the secret to another;
NKJV.10: Lest he who hears it expose your shame, And your reputation be ruined.
NKJV.11: A word fitly spoken is like apples of gold In settings of silver.
NKJV.12: Like an earring of gold and an ornament of fine gold Is a wise rebuker to an obedient ear.
NKJV.13: Like the cold of snow in time of harvest Is a faithful messenger to those who send him, For he refreshes the soul of his masters.
NKJV.14: Whoever falsely boasts of giving Is like clouds and wind without rain.
NKJV.15: By long forbearance a ruler is persuaded, And a gentle tongue breaks a bone.
NKJV.16: Have you found honey? Eat only as much as you need, Lest you be filled with it and vomit.
NKJV.17: Seldom set foot in your neighbor’s house, Lest he become weary of you and hate you.
NKJV.18: A man who bears false witness against his neighbor Is like a club, a sword, and a sharp arrow.
NKJV.19: Confidence in an unfaithful man in time of trouble Is like a bad tooth and a foot out of joint.
NKJV.20: Like one who takes away a garment in cold weather, And like vinegar on soda, Is one who sings songs to a heavy heart.
NKJV.21: If your enemy is hungry, give him bread to eat; And if he is thirsty, give him water to drink;
NKJV.22: For so you will heap coals of fire on his head, And the LORD will reward you.
NKJV.23: The north wind brings forth rain, And a backbiting tongue an angry countenance.
NKJV.24: It is better to dwell in a corner of a housetop, Than in a house shared with a contentious woman.
NKJV.25: As cold water to a weary soul, So is good news from a far country.
NKJV.26: A righteous man who falters before the wicked Is like a murky spring and a polluted well.
NKJV.27: It is not good to eat much honey; So to seek one’s own glory is not glory.
NKJV.28: Whoever has no rule over his own spirit Is like a city broken down, without walls.

KJV.1: These are also proverbs<S>4912</S> of Solomon<S>8010</S>, which the men<S>582</S> of Hezekiah<S>2396</S> king<S>4428</S> of Judah<S>3063</S> copied out<S>6275</S>.

KJV.6: Put not forth<S>1921</S> thyself in the presence<S>6440</S> of the king<S>4428</S>, and stand<S>5975</S> not in the place<S>4725</S> of great<S>1419</S> men: <sup>Put: Heb. Set not out thy glory</sup>

KJV.8: Go not forth<S>3318</S> hastily<S>4118</S> to strive<S>7378</S>, lest thou know not what to do<S>6213</S> in the end<S>319</S> thereof, when thy neighbour<S>7453</S> hath put thee to shame<S>3637</S>.
KJV.9: Debate<S>7378</S> thy cause<S>7379</S> with thy neighbour<S>7453</S> himself; and discover<S>1540</S> not a secret<S>5475</S> to another<S>312</S>: <sup>a secret: or, the secret of</sup>
KJV.10: Lest he that heareth<S>8085</S> it put thee to shame<S>2616</S>, and thine infamy<S>1681</S> turn not away<S>7725</S>.
KJV.11: A word<S>1697</S> fitly<S>212</S> <S>655</S> spoken<S>1696</S> is like apples<S>8598</S> of gold<S>2091</S> in pictures<S>4906</S> of silver<S>3701</S>. <sup>fitly: Heb. spoken upon his wheels</sup>
KJV.12: As an earring<S>5141</S> of gold<S>2091</S>, and an ornament<S>2481</S> of fine gold<S>3800</S>, so is a wise<S>2450</S> reprover<S>3198</S> upon an obedient<S>8085</S> ear<S>241</S>.

KJV.27: It is not good<S>2896</S> to eat<S>398</S> much<S>7235</S> honey<S>1706</S>: so for men to search<S>2714</S> their own glory<S>3519</S> is not glory<S>3519</S>.

ESV.1: These also are proverbs of Solomon which the men of Hezekiah king of Judah copied.
ESV.2: It is the glory of God to conceal things, but the glory of kings is to search things out.
ESV.3: As the heavens for height, and the earth for depth, so the heart of kings is unsearchable.
ESV.4: Take away the dross from the silver, and the smith has material for a vessel;
ESV.5: take away the wicked from the presence of the king, and his throne will be established in righteousness.
ESV.6: Do not put yourself forward in the king's presence or stand in the place of the great,
ESV.7: for it is better to be told, “Come up here,” than to be put lower in the presence of a noble.  What your eyes have seen
ESV.8: do not hastily bring into court,  for  what will you do in the end, when your neighbor puts you to shame?
ESV.9: Argue your case with your neighbor himself, and do not reveal another's secret,
ESV.10: lest he who hears you bring shame upon you, and your ill repute have no end.
ESV.11: A word fitly spoken is like apples of gold in a setting of silver.
ESV.12: Like a gold ring or an ornament of gold is a wise reprover to a listening ear.
ESV.13: Like the cold of snow in the time of harvest is a faithful messenger to those who send him; he refreshes the soul of his masters.
ESV.14: Like clouds and wind without rain is a man who boasts of a gift he does not give.
ESV.15: With patience a ruler may be persuaded, and a soft tongue will break a bone.
ESV.16: If you have found honey, eat only enough for you, lest you have your fill of it and vomit it.
ESV.17: Let your foot be seldom in your neighbor's house, lest he have his fill of you and hate you.
ESV.18: A man who bears false witness against his neighbor is like a war club, or a sword, or a sharp arrow.
ESV.19: Trusting in a treacherous man in time of trouble is like a bad tooth or a foot that slips.
ESV.20: Whoever sings songs to a heavy heart is like one who takes off a garment on a cold day, and like vinegar on soda.
ESV.21: If your enemy is hungry, give him bread to eat, and if he is thirsty, give him water to drink,
ESV.22: for you will heap burning coals on his head, and the LORD will reward you.
ESV.23: The north wind brings forth rain, and a backbiting tongue, angry looks.
ESV.24: It is better to live in a corner of the housetop than in a house shared with a quarrelsome wife.
ESV.25: Like cold water to a thirsty soul, so is good news from a far country.
ESV.26: Like a muddied spring or a polluted fountain is a righteous man who gives way before the wicked.
ESV.27: It is not good to eat much honey, nor is it glorious to seek one's own glory.
ESV.28: A man without self-control is like a city broken into and left without walls.

NASB.1: These also are proverbs of Solomon which the men of Hezekiah, king of Judah, transcribed.
NASB.2: It is the glory of God to conceal a matter, But the glory of kings is to search out a matter.
NASB.3: [As] the heavens for height and the earth for depth, So the heart of kings is unsearchable.
NASB.4: Take away the dross from the silver, And there comes out a vessel for the smith;
NASB.5: Take away the wicked before the king, And his throne will be established in righteousness.
NASB.6: Do not claim honor in the presence of the king, And do not stand in the place of great men;
NASB.7: For it is better that it be said to you, 'Come up here,' Than for you to be placed lower in the presence of the prince, Whom your eyes have seen.
NASB.8: Do not go out hastily to argue [your case]; Otherwise, what will you do in the end, When your neighbor humiliates you?
NASB.9: Argue your case with your neighbor, And do not reveal the secret of another,
NASB.10: Or he who hears [it] will reproach you, And the evil report about you will not pass away.
NASB.11: [Like] apples of gold in settings of silver Is a word spoken in right circumstances.
NASB.12: [Like] an earring of gold and an ornament of fine gold Is a wise reprover to a listening ear.
NASB.13: Like the cold of snow in the time of harvest Is a faithful messenger to those who send him, For he refreshes the soul of his masters.
NASB.14: [Like] clouds and wind without rain Is a man who boasts of his gifts falsely.
NASB.15: By forbearance a ruler may be persuaded, And a soft tongue breaks the bone.
NASB.16: Have you found honey? Eat [only] what you need, That you not have it in excess and vomit it.
NASB.17: Let your foot rarely be in your neighbor's house, Or he will become weary of you and hate you.
NASB.18: [Like] a club and a sword and a sharp arrow Is a man who bears false witness against his neighbor.
NASB.19: [Like] a bad tooth and an unsteady foot Is confidence in a faithless man in time of trouble.
NASB.20: [Like] one who takes off a garment on a cold day, [or like] vinegar on soda, Is he who sings songs to a troubled heart.
NASB.21: If your enemy is hungry, give him food to eat; And if he is thirsty, give him water to drink;
NASB.22: For you will heap burning coals on his head, And the LORD will reward you.
NASB.23: The north wind brings forth rain, And a backbiting tongue, an angry countenance.
NASB.24: It is better to live in a corner of the roof Than in a house shared with a contentious woman.
NASB.25: [Like] cold water to a weary soul, So is good news from a distant land.
NASB.26: [Like] a trampled spring and a polluted well Is a righteous man who gives way before the wicked.
NASB.27: It is not good to eat much honey, Nor is it glory to search out one's own glory.
NASB.28: [Like] a city that is broken into [and] without walls Is a man who has no control over his spirit.

NLT.1: These are more proverbs of Solomon, collected by the advisers of King Hezekiah of Judah.
NLT.2:
It is God’s privilege to conceal things
and the king’s privilege to discover them.
NLT.3:
No one can comprehend the height of heaven, the depth of the earth,
or all that goes on in the king’s mind!
NLT.4:
Remove the impurities from silver,
and the sterling will be ready for the silversmith.
NLT.5:
Remove the wicked from the king’s court,
and his reign will be made secure by justice.
NLT.6:
Don’t demand an audience with the king
or push for a place among the great.
NLT.7:
It’s better to wait for an invitation to the head table
than to be sent away in public disgrace.
Just because you’ve seen something,
NLT.8:
don’t be in a hurry to go to court.
For what will you do in the end
if your neighbor deals you a shameful defeat?
NLT.9:
When arguing with your neighbor,
don’t betray another person’s secret.
NLT.10:
Others may accuse you of gossip,
and you will never regain your good reputation.
NLT.11:
Timely advice is lovely,
like golden apples in a silver basket.
NLT.12:
To one who listens, valid criticism
is like a gold earring or other gold jewelry.
NLT.13:
Trustworthy messengers refresh like snow in summer.
They revive the spirit of their employer.
NLT.14:
A person who promises a gift but doesn’t give it
is like clouds and wind that bring no rain.
NLT.15:
Patience can persuade a prince,
and soft speech can break bones.
NLT.16:
Do you like honey?
Don’t eat too much, or it will make you sick!
NLT.17:
Don’t visit your neighbors too often,
or you will wear out your welcome.
NLT.18:
Telling lies about others
is as harmful as hitting them with an ax,
wounding them with a sword,
or shooting them with a sharp arrow.
NLT.19:
Putting confidence in an unreliable person in times of trouble
is like chewing with a broken tooth or walking on a lame foot.
NLT.20:
Singing cheerful songs to a person with a heavy heart
is like taking someone’s coat in cold weather
or pouring vinegar in a wound.
NLT.21:
If your enemies are hungry, give them food to eat.
If they are thirsty, give them water to drink.
NLT.22:
You will heap burning coals of shame on their heads,
and the LORD will reward you.
NLT.23:
As surely as a north wind brings rain,
so a gossiping tongue causes anger!
NLT.24:
It’s better to live alone in the corner of an attic
than with a quarrelsome wife in a lovely home.
NLT.25:
Good news from far away
is like cold water to the thirsty.
NLT.26:
If the godly give in to the wicked,
it’s like polluting a fountain or muddying a spring.
NLT.27:
It’s not good to eat too much honey,
and it’s not good to seek honors for yourself.
NLT.28:
A person without self-control
is like a city with broken-down walls.

WEB.1: These also are proverbs of Solomon, which the men of Hezekiah king of Judah copied out.
WEB.2: It is the glory of God to conceal a thing, but the glory of kings is to search out a matter.
WEB.3: As the heavens for height, and the earth for depth, so the hearts of kings are unsearchable.
WEB.4: Take away the dross from the silver, and material comes out for the refiner;
WEB.5: Take away the wicked from the king’s presence, and his throne will be established in righteousness.
WEB.6: Don’t exalt yourself in the presence of the king, or claim a place among great men;
WEB.7: for it is better that it be said to you, “Come up here,” than that you should be put lower in the presence of the prince, whom your eyes have seen.
WEB.8: Don’t be hasty in bringing charges to court. What will you do in the end when your neighbor shames you?
WEB.9: Debate your case with your neighbor, and don’t betray the confidence of another;
WEB.10: lest one who hears it put you to shame, and your bad reputation never depart.
WEB.11: A word fitly spoken is like apples of gold in settings of silver.
WEB.12: As an earring of gold, and an ornament of fine gold, so is a wise reprover to an obedient ear.
WEB.13: As the cold of snow in the time of harvest, so is a faithful messenger to those who send him; for he refreshes the soul of his masters.
WEB.14: As clouds and wind without rain, so is he who boasts of gifts deceptively.
WEB.15: By patience a ruler is persuaded. A soft tongue breaks the bone.
WEB.16: Have you found honey? Eat as much as is sufficient for you, lest you eat too much, and vomit it.
WEB.17: Let your foot be seldom in your neighbor’s house, lest he be weary of you, and hate you.
WEB.18: A man who gives false testimony against his neighbor is like a club, a sword, or a sharp arrow.
WEB.19: Confidence in someone unfaithful in time of trouble is like a bad tooth, or a lame foot.
WEB.20: As one who takes away a garment in cold weather, or vinegar on soda, so is one who sings songs to a heavy heart.
WEB.21: If your enemy is hungry, give him food to eat. If he is thirsty, give him water to drink:
WEB.22: for you will heap coals of fire on his head, and Yahweh will reward you.
WEB.23: The north wind produces rain; so a backbiting tongue brings an angry face.
WEB.24: It is better to dwell in the corner of the housetop, than to share a house with a contentious woman.
WEB.25: Like cold water to a thirsty soul, so is good news from a far country.
WEB.26: Like a muddied spring, and a polluted well, so is a righteous man who gives way before the wicked.
WEB.27: It is not good to eat much honey; nor is it honorable to seek one’s own honor.
WEB.28: Like a city that is broken down and without walls is a man whose spirit is without restraint.

CSB17.1:
These too are proverbs of Solomon, ⓛ
which the men of King Hezekiah ⓜ of Judah, copied.
CSB17.2:
It is the glory of God to conceal a matter ⓝ
and the glory of kings to investigate a matter.
CSB17.3:
As the heavens are high and the earth is deep,
so the hearts of kings cannot be investigated.
CSB17.4:
Remove impurities from silver, ⓐ
and material will be produced [1] for a silversmith. [2] ⓑ
CSB17.5:
Remove the wicked from the king’s presence, ⓒ
and his throne will be established in righteousness. ⓓ
CSB17.6:
Don’t boast about yourself before the king,
and don’t stand in the place of the great;
CSB17.7:
for it is better for him to say to you, “Come up here!”
than to demote you in plain view of a noble. [3] ⓔ
CSB17.8:
Don’t take a matter to court hastily. ⓕ
Otherwise, what will you do afterward
if your opponent [4] humiliates you?
CSB17.9:
Make your case with your opponent
without revealing another’s secret; ⓖ
CSB17.10:
otherwise, the one who hears will disgrace you,
and you’ll never live it down. [5]
CSB17.11:
A word spoken at the right time
is like gold apples in silver settings. ⓗ
CSB17.12:
A wise correction to a receptive ear ⓘ
is like a gold ring or an ornament of gold.
CSB17.13:
To those who send him, a trustworthy envoy
is like the coolness of snow on a harvest day;
he refreshes the life of his masters. ⓙ
CSB17.14:
The one who boasts about a gift that does not exist
is like clouds and wind without rain. ⓚ
CSB17.15:
A ruler can be persuaded through patience,
and a gentle tongue can break a bone. ⓛ
CSB17.16:
If you find honey, ⓜ eat only what you need;
otherwise, you’ll get sick from it and vomit. ⓝ
CSB17.17:
Seldom set foot in your neighbor’s house;
otherwise, he’ll get sick of you and hate you.
CSB17.18:
A person giving false testimony against his neighbor ⓐ
is like a club, a sword, or a sharp arrow. ⓑ
CSB17.19:
Trusting an unreliable person in a difficult time
is like a rotten tooth or a faltering foot. ⓒ
CSB17.20:
Singing songs to a troubled heart
is like taking off clothing on a cold day
or like pouring vinegar on soda. [6] ⓓ
CSB17.21:
If your enemy is hungry, give him food to eat,
and if he is thirsty, give him water to drink; ⓔ
CSB17.22:
for you will heap burning coals on his head,
and the LORD will reward you. ⓕ
CSB17.23:
The north wind produces rain,
and a backbiting tongue, angry looks.
CSB17.24:
Better to live on the corner of a roof
than to share a house with a nagging wife. ⓖ
CSB17.25:
Good news from a distant land
is like cold water to a parched throat. [7] ⓗ
CSB17.26:
A righteous person who yields to the wicked ⓘ
is like a muddied spring or a polluted well. ⓙ
CSB17.27:
It is not good to eat too much honey ⓚ
or to seek glory after glory. [8] ⓛ
CSB17.28:
A person who does not control his temper ⓜ
is like a city whose wall is broken down. ⓝ

AMP.1: These are also the proverbs of Solomon, which the men of Hezekiah king of Judah copied:
AMP.2:
It is the glory of God to conceal a matter,
But the glory of kings is to search out a matter.
AMP.3:
As the heavens for height and the earth for depth,
So the hearts and minds of kings are unsearchable.
AMP.4:
Take away the dross from the silver,
And there comes out [the pure metal for] a vessel for the silversmith [to shape].
AMP.5:
Take away the wicked from before the king,
And his throne will be established in righteousness.
AMP.6:
Do not be boastfully ambitious and claim honor in the presence of the king,
And do not stand in the place of great men;
AMP.7:
For it is better that it be said to you, "Come up here,"
Than for you to be placed lower in the presence of the prince,
Whom your eyes have seen.
AMP.8:
Do not rush out to argue your case [before magistrates or judges];
Otherwise what will you do in the end [when your case is lost and]
When your neighbor (opponent) humiliates you?
AMP.9:
Argue your case with your neighbor himself [before you go to court];
And do not reveal another's secret,
AMP.10:
Or he who hears it will shame you
And the rumor about you [and your action in court] will have no end.
AMP.11:
Like apples of gold in settings of silver
Is a word spoken at the right time.
AMP.12:
Like an earring of gold and an ornament of fine gold
Is a wise reprover to an ear that listens and learns.
AMP.13:
Like the cold of snow [brought from the mountains] in the time of harvest,
So is a faithful messenger to those who send him;
For he refreshes the life of his masters.
AMP.14:
Like clouds and wind without rain
Is a man who boasts falsely of gifts [he does not give].
AMP.15:
By patience and a calm spirit a ruler may be persuaded,
And a soft and gentle tongue breaks the bone [of resistance].
AMP.16:
Have you found [pleasure sweet like] honey? Eat only as much as you need,
Otherwise, being filled excessively, you vomit it.
AMP.17:
Let your foot seldom be in your neighbor's house,
Or he will become tired of you and hate you.
AMP.18:
Like a club and a sword and a piercing arrow
Is a man who testifies falsely against his neighbor (acquaintance).
AMP.19:
Like a broken tooth or an unsteady foot
Is confidence in an unfaithful man in time of trouble.
AMP.20:
Like one who takes off a garment in cold weather, or like [a reactive, useless mixture of] vinegar on soda,
Is he who [thoughtlessly] sings [joyful] songs to a heavy heart.
AMP.21:
If your enemy is hungry, give him bread to eat;
And if he is thirsty, give him water to drink;
AMP.22:
For in doing so, you will heap coals of fire upon his head,
And the LORD will reward you.
AMP.23:
The north wind brings forth rain;
And a backbiting tongue, an angry countenance.
AMP.24:
It is better to live in a corner of the housetop [on the flat roof, exposed to the weather]
Than in a house shared with a quarrelsome (contentious) woman.
AMP.25:
Like cold water to a thirsty soul,
So is good news from a distant land.
AMP.26:
Like a muddied fountain and a polluted spring
Is a righteous man who yields and compromises his integrity before the wicked.
AMP.27:
It is not good to eat much honey,
Nor is it glorious to seek one's own glory.
AMP.28:
Like a city that is broken down and without walls [leaving it unprotected]
Is a man who has no self-control over his spirit [and sets himself up for trouble].

MSG.1:
There are also these proverbs of Solomon,
collected by scribes of Hezekiah, king of Judah.
MSG.2:
God delights in concealing things;
scientists delight in discovering things.
MSG.3:
Like the horizons for breadth and the ocean for depth,
the understanding of a good leader is broad and deep.
MSG.4:
Remove impurities from the silver
and the silversmith can craft a fine chalice;
MSG.5:
Remove the wicked from leadership
and authority will be credible and God-honoring.
MSG.6:
Don't work yourself into the spotlight;
don't push your way into the place of prominence.
MSG.7:
It's better to be promoted to a place of honor
than face humiliation by being demoted.
MSG.8:
Don't jump to conclusions—there may be
a perfectly good explanation for what you just saw.
MSG.9:
In the heat of an argument,
don't betray confidences;
MSG.10:
Word is sure to get around,
and no one will trust you.
MSG.11:
The right word at the right time
is like a custom-made piece of jewelry,
MSG.12:
And a wise friend's timely reprimand
is like a gold ring slipped on your finger.
MSG.13:
Reliable friends who do what they say
are like cool drinks in sweltering heat—refreshing!
MSG.14:
Like billowing clouds that bring no rain
is the person who talks big but never produces.
MSG.15:
Patient persistence pierces through indifference;
gentle speech breaks down rigid defenses.
MSG.16:
When you're given a box of candy, don't gulp it all down;
eat too much chocolate and you'll make yourself sick;
MSG.17:
And when you find a friend, don't outwear your welcome;
show up at all hours and he'll soon get fed up.
MSG.18:
Anyone who tells lies against the neighbors
in court or on the street is a loose cannon.
MSG.19:
Trusting a double-crosser when you're in trouble
is like biting down on an abscessed tooth.
MSG.20:
Singing light songs to the heavyhearted
is like pouring salt in their wounds.
MSG.21:
If you see your enemy hungry, go buy him lunch;
if he's thirsty, bring him a drink.
MSG.22:
Your generosity will surprise him with goodness,
and God will look after you.
MSG.23:
A north wind brings stormy weather,
and a gossipy tongue stormy looks.
MSG.24:
Better to live alone in a tumbledown shack
than share a mansion with a nagging spouse.
MSG.25:
Like a cool drink of water when you're worn out and weary
is a letter from a long-lost friend.
MSG.26:
A good person who gives in to a bad person
is a muddied spring, a polluted well.
MSG.27:
It's not smart to stuff yourself with sweets,
nor is glory piled on glory good for you.
MSG.28:
A person without self-control
is like a house with its doors and windows knocked out.

NIV.1:
More Proverbs of Solomon
These are more proverbs of Solomon, copied by the men of Hezekiah king of Judah:
NIV.2:
It is the glory of God to conceal a matter;
to search out a matter is the glory of kings.
NIV.3:
As the heavens are high and the earth is deep,
so the hearts of kings are unsearchable.
NIV.4:
Remove the dross from the silver,
and out comes material for the silversmith;
NIV.5:
remove the wicked from the king’s presence,
and his throne will be established through righteousness.
NIV.6:
Do not exalt yourself in the king’s presence,
and do not claim a place among great men;
NIV.7:
it is better for him to say to you, “Come up here,”
than for him to humiliate you before a nobleman.
What you have seen with your eyes
NIV.8:
do not bring hastily to court,
for what will you do in the end
if your neighbor puts you to shame?
NIV.9:
If you argue your case with a neighbor,
do not betray another man’s confidence,
NIV.10:
or he who hears it may shame you
and you will never lose your bad reputation.
NIV.11:
A word aptly spoken
is like apples of gold in settings of silver.
NIV.12:
Like an earring of gold or an ornament of fine gold
is a wise man’s rebuke to a listening ear.
NIV.13:
Like the coolness of snow at harvest time
is a trustworthy messenger to those who send him;
he refreshes the spirit of his masters.
NIV.14:
Like clouds and wind without rain
is a man who boasts of gifts he does not give.
NIV.15:
Through patience a ruler can be persuaded,
and a gentle tongue can break a bone.
NIV.16:
If you find honey, eat just enough—
too much of it, and you will vomit.
NIV.17:
Seldom set foot in your neighbor’s house—
too much of you, and he will hate you.
NIV.18:
Like a club or a sword or a sharp arrow
is the man who gives false testimony against his neighbor.
NIV.19:
Like a bad tooth or a lame foot
is reliance on the unfaithful in times of trouble.
NIV.20:
Like one who takes away a garment on a cold day,
or like vinegar poured on soda,
is one who sings songs to a heavy heart.
NIV.21:
If your enemy is hungry, give him food to eat;
if he is thirsty, give him water to drink.
NIV.22:
In doing this, you will heap burning coals on his head,
and the Lord will reward you.
NIV.23:
As a north wind brings rain,
so a sly tongue brings angry looks.
NIV.24:
Better to live on a corner of the roof
than share a house with a quarrelsome wife.
NIV.25:
Like cold water to a weary soul
is good news from a distant land.
NIV.26:
Like a muddied spring or a polluted well
is a righteous man who gives way to the wicked.
NIV.27:
It is not good to eat too much honey,
nor is it honorable to seek one’s own honor.
NIV.28:
Like a city whose walls are broken down
is a man who lacks self-control.

NRSVCE.1: These are other proverbs of Solomon that the officials of King Hezekiah of Judah copied.
NRSVCE.2:
It is the glory of God to conceal things,
but the glory of kings is to search things out.
NRSVCE.3:
Like the heavens for height, like the earth for depth,
so the mind of kings is unsearchable.
NRSVCE.4:
Take away the dross from the silver,
and the smith has material for a vessel;
NRSVCE.5:
take away the wicked from the presence of the king,
and his throne will be established in righteousness.
NRSVCE.6:
Do not put yourself forward in the king’s presence
or stand in the place of the great;
NRSVCE.7:
for it is better to be told, “Come up here,”
than to be put lower in the presence of a noble.
What your eyes have seen
NRSVCE.8:
do not hastily bring into court;
for what will you do in the end,
when your neighbor puts you to shame?
NRSVCE.9:
Argue your case with your neighbor directly,
and do not disclose another’s secret;
NRSVCE.10:
or else someone who hears you will bring shame upon you,
and your ill repute will have no end.
NRSVCE.11:
A word fitly spoken
is like apples of gold in a setting of silver.
NRSVCE.12:
Like a gold ring or an ornament of gold
is a wise rebuke to a listening ear.
NRSVCE.13:
Like the cold of snow in the time of harvest
are faithful messengers to those who send them;
they refresh the spirit of their masters.
NRSVCE.14:
Like clouds and wind without rain
is one who boasts of a gift never given.
NRSVCE.15:
With patience a ruler may be persuaded,
and a soft tongue can break bones.
NRSVCE.16:
If you have found honey, eat only enough for you,
or else, having too much, you will vomit it.
NRSVCE.17:
Let your foot be seldom in your neighbor’s house,
otherwise the neighbor will become weary of you and hate you.
NRSVCE.18:
Like a war club, a sword, or a sharp arrow
is one who bears false witness against a neighbor.
NRSVCE.19:
Like a bad tooth or a lame foot
is trust in a faithless person in time of trouble.
NRSVCE.20:
Like vinegar on a wound
is one who sings songs to a heavy heart.
Like a moth in clothing or a worm in wood,
sorrow gnaws at the human heart.
NRSVCE.21:
If your enemies are hungry, give them bread to eat;
and if they are thirsty, give them water to drink;
NRSVCE.22:
for you will heap coals of fire on their heads,
and the LORD will reward you.
NRSVCE.23:
The north wind produces rain,
and a backbiting tongue, angry looks.
NRSVCE.24:
It is better to live in a corner of the housetop
than in a house shared with a contentious wife.
NRSVCE.25:
Like cold water to a thirsty soul,
so is good news from a far country.
NRSVCE.26:
Like a muddied spring or a polluted fountain
are the righteous who give way before the wicked.
NRSVCE.27:
It is not good to eat much honey,
or to seek honor on top of honor.
NRSVCE.28:
Like a city breached, without walls,
is one who lacks self-control.

NET.1: These also are proverbs of Solomon, which the men of King Hezekiah of Judah copied:
NET.2: It is the glory of God to conceal a matter, and it is the glory of a king to search out a matter.
NET.3: As the heaven is high and the earth is deep so the hearts of kings are unsearchable.
NET.4: Remove the dross from the silver, and material for the silversmith will emerge;
NET.5: remove the wicked from before the king, and his throne will be established in righteousness.
NET.6: Do not honor yourself before the king, and do not stand in the place of great men;
NET.7: for it is better for him to say to you, “Come up here,” than to put you lower before a prince, whom your eyes have seen.
NET.8: Do not go out hastily to litigation, or what will you do afterward when your neighbor puts you to shame?
NET.9: When you argue a case with your neighbor, do not reveal the secret of another person,
NET.10: lest the one who hears it put you to shame and your infamy will never go away.
NET.11: Like apples of gold in settings of silver, so is a word skillfully spoken.
NET.12: Like an earring of gold and an ornament of fine gold, so is a wise reprover to the ear of the one who listens.
NET.13: Like the cold of snow in the time of harvest, so is a faithful messenger to those who send him, for he refreshes the heart of his masters.
NET.14: Like cloudy skies and wind that produce no rain, so is the one who boasts of a gift not given.
NET.15: Through patience a ruler can be persuaded, and a soft tongue can break a bone.
NET.16: When you find honey, eat only what is sufficient for you, lest you become stuffed with it and vomit it up.
NET.17: Don’t set foot too frequently in your neighbor’s house, lest he become weary of you and hate you.
NET.18: Like a club or a sword or a sharp arrow, so is the one who testifies against his neighbor as a false witness.
NET.19: Like a bad tooth or a foot out of joint, so is confidence in an unfaithful person at the time of trouble.
NET.20: Like one who takes off a garment on a cold day, or like vinegar poured on soda, so is one who sings songs to a heavy heart.
NET.21: If your enemy is hungry, give him food to eat, and if he is thirsty, give him water to drink,
NET.22: for you will heap coals of fire on his head, and the LORD will reward you.
NET.23: The north wind brings forth rain, and a gossiping tongue brings forth an angry look.
NET.24: It is better to live on a corner of the housetop than in a house in company with a quarrelsome wife.
NET.25: Like cold water to a weary person, so is good news from a distant land.
NET.26: Like a muddied spring and a polluted well, so is a righteous person who gives way before the wicked.
NET.27: It is not good to eat too much honey, nor is it honorable for people to seek their own glory.
NET.28: Like a city that is broken down and without a wall, so is a person who cannot control his temper.
